chunk_summary: Functional dysphagia risk stratification combines GMFCS and EDACS to assess safety, efficiency, and respiratory vulnerability in neurodisability feeding support, with specific tools for ages 3+ and 18-36 months.
learning_objectives: Understand the EDACS classification system for evaluating eating and drinking safety and efficiency.
Learn how GMFCS levels correlate with dysphagia and aspiration risk in neurodisability patients.
Apply risk stratification guidelines to identify low, moderate, and high-risk feeding scenarios.
Explore the use of Mini-EDACS for early dysphagia assessment in children aged 18-36 months.
key_facts: EDACS levels range from I (safe and efficient) to V (unsafe, tube feeding likely needed).
Mini-EDACS is designed for ages 18-36 months to assess developing swallowing and texture handling.
High GMFCS levels (IV-V) are associated with increased dysphagia and aspiration risk due to factors like scoliosis and ineffective cough.
Low risk: EDACS I-II with GMFCS I-III and no recurrent respiratory infections.
Moderate risk: EDACS III or EDACS II with recurrent infections, prolonged meals (>30-40 minutes), or weight faltering.
High risk: EDACS IV-V or any level with cyanosis, oxygen desaturation, or suspected aspiration pneumonia during feeding.
reference_quote: EDACS classifies usual eating and drinking performance (from age 3 years) by safety (choking and aspiration risk) and efficiency (time, effort, oral loss): level I is safe and efficient; level V cannot eat or drink safely and tube feeding may be needed.
document_key_topics: EDACS classification system for dysphagia risk
GMFCS levels and their impact on feeding safety
Risk stratification for neurodisability feeding support
Mini-EDACS for early pediatric dysphagia assessment
